item.title: [Psychology] — Weekly digest 2026-06-13 to 2026-06-20
item.dc:creator: AI Categorizer
item.description:
Psychology

The articles in this category explore profound aspects of human consciousness, selfhood, and mental states. Oliver Sacks highlights the necessity of "illusions" and diverse states of consciousness, suggesting these provide crucial detachment from time and mortality, expanding our understanding beyond mere waking awareness. The concept of identity is further explored through Amin Maalouf's lens, where selfhood is an integrated "parchment" defined by intentions and choices, not fragmented traits, contradicting societal pressures to present only "slices" of oneself. Ursula K. Le Guin offers a compelling definition of maturity as the courageous act of embracing and integrating one's past selves with compassion, rather than merely aging. Finally, the article on Kafka connects insomnia not just to suffering but to its paradoxical role as a "crucible" for creativity, learning, and emotional regulation, suggesting that even disruptive mental states can foster profound human capacities. These pieces collectively emphasize the dynamic, complex, and often counter-intuitive nature of the human mind and personal development.

  • ★★★ Insomnia and the Secret Life of Ideas: Kafka on the Relationship Between Sleeplessness and Creativity — The Marginalian
    Insomnia, often a source of torment, also serves as a potent crucible for creativity and self-reflection, as seen in Kafka's experiences.
    Insomnia, despite its torment, is portrayed as a crucial yet maddening aspect of human consciousness that intertwines with creativity, learning, and emotional regulation. Kafka's experience highlights the mind's mysterious refusal to sleep, leading to intense rumination and a profound fissure between conscious desire and unconscious will. The article suggests that this state, while difficult, acts as a crucible for our most profound human capacities.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ The Meaning of Maturity: Ursula K. Le Guin on What It Really Takes to Grow Up — The Marginalian
    True maturity involves courageous self-compassion, integrating past selves, and taking tender responsibility for one's entire journey, rather than merely growing older.
    True maturity is defined not by physical growth or increased responsibilities, but by the courage to embrace and integrate all past selves with compassion. Ursula K. Le Guin's perspective suggests that prior versions of oneself are not outgrown but remain within the present person. Without tender responsibility for past missteps and confusions, one cannot fully own their current identity or actualize future potential.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Oliver Sacks on the Necessity of Our Illusions — The Marginalian
    Human consciousness encompasses various states beyond normal waking, and illusions can be essential for coping with time, mortality, and finding necessary detachment.
    Oliver Sacks, influenced by William James, emphasized that normal waking consciousness is merely one form of experience, alongside many other potential states. The article highlights that altered states of consciousness, including those induced by hunger or hormonal shifts, are common and necessary. These "transports" and even illusions provide essential detachment from the relentless awareness of time and mortality, aiding in our ability to cope with existence.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ What Makes a Person: The Seven Layers of Selfhood in Literature and Life — The Marginalian
    A person's identity is a complex, integrated whole defined by intentions and choices, not merely a collection of traits, despite societal pressures to compartmentalize selfhood.
    A person's identity is an integrated whole, likened to a tightly stretched parchment where touching one allegiance affects the entire self. The article, referencing Amin Maalouf, argues that a person is defined more by their intentions and capacities for choice than by a mere configuration of traits. It criticizes modern pressures to present fragmented versions of oneself, suggesting this lacerates the inherent wholeness of identity.
    Read more →

item.title: [Philosophy] — Weekly digest 2026-06-13 to 2026-06-20
item.dc:creator: AI Categorizer
item.description:
Philosophy

This collection of articles delves into the profound philosophical questions of life's meaning, mortality, and the human condition. Oliver Sacks, echoing Albert Camus, posits that meaning is not discovered but actively created, particularly through the difficult yet transformative process of converting despair into love. This sentiment resonates with Fyodor Dostoevsky's realization, following his commuted death sentence, that life's core task is to maintain one's humanity and resilience despite overwhelming circumstances. The theme of mortality as a catalyst for meaning is central, with the argument that accepting loss and our inevitable return to the universe, as articulated by Elizabeth Bishop and William Blake, fosters creativity and defines life's essence. The Samurai perspective reinforces this by suggesting that truly living involves confronting and accepting death daily, as denial of mortality ultimately means a denial of life itself. These articles collectively argue that human flourishing and the pursuit of meaning are deeply intertwined with our capacity to confront, embrace, and transcend life's inherent suffering, loss, and finitude.

  • ★★★ Oliver Sacks on Despair and the Meaning of Life — The Marginalian
    Meaning in life is not found but actively created through the transmutation of despair into love and an engaged search for purpose.
    Oliver Sacks believed that the meaning of life is intrinsically linked to love, which is not discovered but actively constructed. This construction often occurs in times of despair and confusion, transforming shattered hopes into purpose. Citing Albert Camus, the article posits that there is no love of life without despair, making the active transmutation of despair into love the essence of meaning-making.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Necessary Losses: The Life-Shaping Art of Letting Go — The Marginalian
    Accepting loss and mortality is fundamental to human existence, serving as a powerful catalyst for creativity and the discovery of life's meaning.
    The art of letting go, of mastering loss, is presented as an inherent aspect of human existence, with mortality being the evolutionary force ensuring species survival. The article argues that human creativity and productivity, from poetry to science, are ultimately coping mechanisms for the elemental knowledge of inevitable loss. It suggests that life's meaning is found precisely in how we embrace and process these necessary losses.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Dostoyevsky, Just After His Death Sentence Was Repealed, on the Meaning of Life — The Marginalian
    The essence of life lies in remaining authentically human and resilient in all circumstances, never yielding to despondency, as Dostoevsky realized after facing a death sentence.
    Fyodor Dostoevsky, reflecting on his life after a commuted death sentence, concluded that the true task of life is to remain a human being among people, never losing heart or growing despondent, regardless of circumstances. His experience underscored the importance of resilience and an unwavering commitment to one's humanity. This profound insight emerged from facing the ultimate loss and being granted a second chance at life.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ How to Live Fully: The Samurai Guide to Dying Every Day — The Marginalian
    To live fully requires confronting and accepting mortality daily, as denying death ultimately means denying life itself.
    The human paradox lies in our mortality driving our search for meaning, yet we often deny this fundamental truth. The article posits that denying death ultimately prevents one from living fully, echoing Rilke's view that death brings an "absolute and passionate presence" to life. Learning to "die every day," as the Samurai tradition suggests, encourages a profound acceptance of impermanence, making each moment more vibrant and meaningful.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Simone de Beauvoir on How Chance and Choice Converge to Make Us Who We Are — The Marginalian
    Simone de Beauvoir's perspective posits that individual identity is a dynamic product of both predetermined circumstances and deliberate personal decisions.
    De Beauvoir argues that an individual's life embodies the confluence of cosmic history and personal agency, where identity is forged through an interplay of chance events and conscious choices. She suggests that moments of profound personal decision or serendipity can redirect one's path, shaping the unique person one becomes by navigating the tension between external influences and internal will. This process of self-creation is a continuous marvel, a "Rube Goldberg machine" of becoming.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ How to Be More Alive: Hermann Hesse on Wonder and the Proper Aim of Education — The Marginalian
    Hermann Hesse argues that cultivating a sense of wonder is essential for experiencing life's unity and overcoming separation, forming the true objective of education.
    Hermann Hesse believes that wonder is the fundamental conduit to experiencing the world's underlying unity, transcending the feeling of separation. He asserts that this capacity for wonder is scale-agnostic, discoverable in both the mundane and the magnificent, echoing Walt Whitman's view that a blade of grass is as profound as the stars. Hesse implies that a genuine education should not merely transmit facts but foster this innate ability to perceive and appreciate the miraculous in reality, enabling a fuller, more "alive" existence.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ A Spell Against Stagnation: John O’Donohue on Beginnings — The Marginalian
    John O’Donohue contends that embracing new beginnings is a vital act of courage that counters stagnation and unlocks a person's full potential, as life itself depends on continuous initiation.
    John O’Donohue posits that human life is inherently a process of continuous becoming, reliant on the courage to embrace new beginnings to ward off stagnation. He suggests that embarking on a new endeavor, relationship, or practice acts as a "spell" against inertia, revealing both immense power and vulnerability. These pivotal moments challenge individuals to reconfigure their life narratives, inviting them into a symphony of possibilities that can only be accessed by breaking existing patterns.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Nick Cave on the Two Pillars of a Meaningful Life — The Marginalian
    Nick Cave advises that a meaningful life is built upon two core principles: cultivating a questioning mind through conversation and embracing open-hearted qualities like kindness and vulnerability over cynicism.
    Nick Cave suggests that to navigate a seemingly chaotic world and find purpose, individuals must nurture two key spiritual qualities. The first is a "questioning mind," primarily fostered through open conversation, which enriches one's engagement with the world. The second involves actively cultivating kindness, wonder, and vulnerability, consciously turning away from the destructive influence of cynicism. By tending to these inner attributes, one can create a meaningful existence that aligns with higher human values.
    Read more →

item.title: [Other] — Weekly digest 2026-06-13 to 2026-06-20
item.dc:creator: AI Categorizer
item.description:
Other

The article featuring Jeanne Villepreux-Power highlights the extraordinary journey of a pioneering self-taught naturalist in the 19th century. Her story details overcoming significant personal adversity, including assault and professional setbacks, to make groundbreaking scientific contributions. Villepreux-Power's persistent observation and experimentation led to solving the mystery of the argonaut and her invention of the first functional aquarium, which revolutionized the study of marine life in controlled environments. Her work laid crucial groundwork for understanding cephalopod intelligence and earned her lasting recognition in the scientific community despite the societal challenges she faced as a woman in science.

  • ★★★ The Seamstress Who Solved the Ancient Mystery of the Argonaut, Pioneered the Aquarium, and Laid the Groundwork for the Study of Octopus Intelligence — The Marginalian
    Jeanne Villepreux-Power, a pioneering 19th-century self-taught naturalist, overcame immense personal adversity to solve the mystery of the paper nautilus and invent the aquarium, significantly advancing marine biology.
    Jeanne Villepreux-Power, an extraordinary 19th-century figure, embarked on a remarkable journey from a rural seamstress to a pivotal scientific innovator despite facing personal assault and professional setbacks. Driven by persistence, she dedicated months to rigorous observation and experimentation, ultimately uncovering the reproductive secrets of the argonaut (paper nautilus). Her invention of the first functional aquarium revolutionized the study of marine life in controlled environments, laying foundational work for understanding cephalopod intelligence and earning her recognition in the scientific community.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Will China, Inc. be zombified? — Noahpinion
    China's current economic trajectory raises concerns that its state-backed corporate sector could follow Japan's post-bubble path, leading to widespread "zombie companies" sustained by artificial credit.
    The article draws a cautious comparison between China's current economic situation and Japan's post-bubble "lost decades," particularly regarding the potential for "zombie companies." It highlights how Daiei, once Japan's top retailer, became a famous zombie company kept alive by bank loans after Japan's asset bubble burst. While acknowledging significant differences between the Chinese and Japanese economies and global contexts, the piece raises the critical question of whether China's industrial policies and debt-fueled growth could lead to a similar phenomenon of unproductive companies being propped up, thereby hindering broader economic recovery and innovation. The author emphasizes the need to consider both similarities and crucial distinctions when making such historical comparisons.
    Read more →
  • ★★ The Inventor of the Thinking Machine Didn’t Worry. Neither Should You — Nautilus
    Concerns about modern AI's potential dangers are overblown, echoing historical anxieties that even the creators of early "thinking machines" did not share.
    Contemporary fears surrounding artificial intelligence are often fueled by a misunderstanding of its nature and historical context. This article argues that the original developers of concepts related to "thinking machines" did not exhibit the same level of existential dread, suggesting that current anxieties about AI displacing human intelligence or posing an existential threat might be disproportionate and rooted in a lack of historical perspective.
    Read more →
  • ★★ The Model for Botticelli’s Venus Died at 23 — Nautilus
    Researchers propose a new theory for the premature death of the historical model for Botticelli’s Venus, Simonetta Vespucci, at the age of 23.
    Simonetta Vespucci, widely believed to be the muse for Sandro Botticelli's iconic "The Birth of Venus," tragically died at 23. New research challenges existing assumptions about her demise, offering a fresh hypothesis. This investigation into historical medical records and contemporary accounts seeks to uncover the precise cause of her untimely death, providing deeper insight into the lives of figures central to Renaissance art and culture.
    Read more →

item.title: [Arts & Culture] — Weekly digest 2026-06-13 to 2026-06-20
item.dc:creator: AI Categorizer
item.description:
Arts & Culture

The evolving landscape of media and artistic legacy is explored through film analysis and literary archiving. Jake Flanagin's critique of 'The Devil Wears Prada 2' highlights how contemporary cultural shifts, driven by HR concerns and advertiser sensitivities, are re-shaping iconic characters like Miranda Priestly, even as the film subtly betrays a nostalgia for the earlier era's ruthless efficiency. Concurrently, Mehrdad Babadi examines how Iranian director Jafar Panahi's recent film, 'It Was Just an Accident', develops a brief moment of anxiety from his 2015 work 'Taxi' into a profound exploration of state surveillance and its psychological toll, demonstrating how artists re-engage with past themes to reflect ongoing sociopolitical realities. Liz Cettina's discussion of Willa Cather's archives parallels this by illustrating how literary legacies, much like Cather's protagonist Thea Kronborg's success documented in a scrapbook, are constructed and preserved, revealing layers of personal ambition and societal recognition within an artist's body of work. Together, these pieces examine how creative works respond to societal change, how cultural memories are processed, and how artists confront and re-interpret themes of power, fear, and legacy.

  • ★★ ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’ wants to rehabilitate the fashion industry’s brutal early 2000s workplace culture, but it can’t quite hide how much it misses it — 3 Quarks Daily
    The sequel to 'The Devil Wears Prada' attempts to soften Miranda Priestly's brutal persona, yet it unintentionally highlights a nostalgic yearning for the ruthless efficiency of early 2000s fashion workplaces.
    The 'The Devil Wears Prada 2' sequel dilutes Miranda Priestly's iconic icy authority, attributing her diminished power to human resources complaints, advertiser sensitivities, and the erosion of legacy media's influence. While the film tries to rehabilitate the early 2000s workplace culture it depicted, its portrayal of a 'softened' Miranda paradoxically reveals a lingering admiration for the uncompromising, albeit brutal, efficiency of that era. This shift reflects broader changes in corporate culture and media power dynamics.
    Read more →
  • ★★ The Fate Of Willa Cather’s Archives — 3 Quarks Daily
    Willa Cather's literary archives reveal a complex narrative of identity and ambition, mirroring her protagonist Thea Kronborg's journey through scrapbooks that document celebrity and personal achievement.
    Willa Cather's 1915 novel, 'The Song of the Lark', concludes with protagonist Thea Kronborg's success as a singer being meticulously documented in a scrapbook, reflecting the vicarious ambition of her aunt Tillie. This literary device of collecting news clippings about celebrities and then about Thea herself serves as a tangible representation of realized dreams. The article suggests that Cather's own archives likely echo this theme, offering insights into her creative process and personal reflections on success and legacy.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Panahi’s Laboratory — 3 Quarks Daily
    Jafar Panahi's recent film 'It Was Just an Accident' revisits a fleeting moment of fear from his 2015 film 'Taxi', transforming a brief encounter with state surveillance into a central thematic concern.
    Iranian director Jafar Panahi's new film, 'It Was Just an Accident', develops a passing fear from his earlier work, 'Taxi', into a central exploration of state oppression and psychological trauma. In 'Taxi', Panahi's character experiences a momentary panic, believing he recognized the voice of his interrogator, a common reported effect among human rights lawyer Nasrin Sotoudeh's clients. A decade later, Panahi expands this fleeting glimpse into the pervasive and haunting impact of surveillance and interrogation on the individual psyche.
    Read more →

item.title: [Science] — Weekly digest 2026-06-13 to 2026-06-20
item.dc:creator: AI Categorizer
item.description:
Science

Recent scientific advancements are pushing the boundaries of human understanding and technological application in medicine and warfare. Max Kozlov reports on groundbreaking neuroscience research, where Ziv Williams and his team tracked individual brain cells in the human frontotemporal cortex, discovering that specific neurons act as specialized linguistic building blocks for grammar, vocabulary, and meaning just before speech, challenging previous diffuse network theories of language. In the medical field, Joshua Cohen evaluates the promise of multi-cancer early detection (MCED) "liquid biopsies," which aim to revolutionize cancer diagnosis by identifying early signals from a single blood draw. While these tests offer the potential to detect cancers at more treatable stages, Cohen cautions that they may not yet be ready for widespread clinical adoption despite industry-funded research. Concurrently, Nathan Gardels reveals how the war in Ukraine has transformed geopolitical conflict, becoming the first to extensively use AI-assisted precision-guided drones. This shift enables strikes deep into enemy territory, marking a new era where technological sophistication dictates battlefield outcomes and fundamentally alters the nature of modern warfare, underscoring the rapid integration of advanced technology into global power dynamics.

  • ★★★ How the brain builds sentences, neuron by neuron — 3 Quarks Daily
    Individual brain cells in the human frontotemporal cortex act as specialized linguistic building blocks, precisely weaving grammar, vocabulary, and meaning in the fraction of a second before speech.
    New research, published in Nature, tracked the electrical activity of individual brain cells during unscripted conversations, revealing how sentences are constructed before being spoken. Scientists, including neurosurgeon Ziv Williams, discovered that specific neurons in the frontotemporal cortex are specialized for linguistic tasks. This finding challenges the previous notion that language processing is a diffuse, whole-network phenomenon, instead showing that distinct brain cells contribute to grammar, vocabulary, and meaning.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ The Warfare Of The Future Is Already Here — 3 Quarks Daily
    The war in Ukraine has transformed warfare into an era of AI-assisted precision-guided drones, fundamentally changing military strategy and projecting conflict deep into enemy territory.
    The conflict in Ukraine has reshaped modern warfare by becoming the first to extensively deploy AI-assisted precision-guided drones, altering conventional military dynamics. Despite Russia's initial military advantage, Ukraine has effectively used this technology to strike deep into Russian territory, including targets 1,000 miles from Kyiv. This development signifies a new era of warfare where technological sophistication, rather than sheer military might, dictates battlefield outcomes and allows for unprecedented long-range precision strikes.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Promising ‘Liquid Biopsies’ May Not Be Ready for Primetime — 3 Quarks Daily
    Multi-cancer early detection (MCED) "liquid biopsies" show promise for revolutionizing cancer diagnosis by identifying early signs from a single blood draw, but their readiness for widespread clinical use remains uncertain.
    Multi-cancer early detection (MCED) tests, a type of liquid biopsy, aim to transform cancer diagnosis by detecting cancer signals like circulating tumor DNA from a single body fluid sample. Supporters highlight their potential to identify cancers at earlier, more treatable stages, which could save lives. However, despite some promising simulation research funded by industry players, experts like Joshua Cohen caution that these tests may not yet be fully ready for widespread adoption or routine clinical practice.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Why the Human Genome’s Tangled Physicality May Confound AI — Quanta Magazine
    The human genome's physical, 3D structure and dynamic interactions, not just its linear sequence, are crucial for its function and may present a significant challenge for AI-based analysis.
    Biologists have historically viewed DNA as a static blueprint, but its physical, three-dimensional organization and dynamic interactions within the cell are fundamental to its function. This complex physicality, involving dynamic looping and protein interactions, adds layers of information beyond the linear genetic code. Artificial intelligence, currently focused on sequence analysis, may struggle to model and understand these intricate physical and temporal aspects. A deeper comprehension of the genome's "tangled physicality" is necessary for truly unlocking its secrets, and current AI approaches might be insufficient.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ How Many Elementary Particles Are There, Really? — Quanta Magazine
    Counting elementary particles involves complex considerations, as their existence is inferred from experiments like those at the Large Hadron Collider, and the Standard Model only accounts for a fraction of known particles.
    Determining the true number of elementary particles is more complex than a simple count, as it involves distinguishing between experimentally confirmed particles, theorized particles, and their various states. The Standard Model identifies 17 elementary particles (12 fermions, 4 bosons, plus the Higgs boson), but this doesn't include gravitons, dark matter particles, or numerous composite particles. Physicists must reconcile theoretical frameworks with experimental observations to refine our understanding of these fundamental constituents of matter and energy. The question of "how many" depends on how one defines and categorizes these elusive entities.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Never Cross a River Four Feet Deep on Average — Astral Codex Ten
    A replication study failed to confirm that flashing lights at a person's individual peak alpha frequency enhances perceptual learning, challenging previous findings on brain rhythm coordination.
    A 2024 ACX Grants project attempted to replicate a 2023 study claiming that individualized light entrainment at a person's peak alpha frequency could boost perceptual learning. The original study suggested that learning depends on the brain's rhythmic coordination and that external light could act as a metronome. However, the replication effort did not confirm these findings, raising questions about the robustness of the initial claim and emphasizing the importance of independent scientific verification in understanding cognitive processes.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Preliminary Thoughts On The Midjourney Scanner — Astral Codex Ten
    Midjourney, an AI image generation company, is pivoting to healthcare with a new "MidJourney Scanner" that promises rapid, radiation-free ultrasound tomography for detailed 3D body imaging.
    Midjourney, known for its AI image model, announced a significant pivot into medical technology with its new "MidJourney Scanner." This device aims to revolutionize healthcare diagnostics by using ultrasound tomography, where a patient is immersed in water and scanned from multiple angles to create a detailed 3D image of the body. The technology promises to deliver rich data comparable to CT or MRI scans in just twenty seconds, without the harmful radiation associated with traditional methods, representing a bold application of AI in health.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Can “Dante’s Inferno” Tell Us Something About Space Rocks? — Nautilus
    Dante's Inferno, a medieval literary work, may offer insights into real-world phenomena like space rocks through the lens of geomythology.
    Geomythology explores connections between ancient myths and geological events. An expert conversation suggests that Dante's Inferno, a literary classic, could potentially be interpreted to contain observations or reflections related to celestial objects or impacts, implying a historical record of such events embedded in cultural narratives. This approach leverages interdisciplinary study to glean scientific understanding from seemingly unrelated texts.
    Read more →
  • ★★ How to Dodge a Mountain Lion — Nautilus
    New research provides updated strategies for minimizing dangerous encounters with mountain lions in California's mountainous regions.
    A recent study re-evaluates puma-human interactions in California, offering insights into their behavior and how humans can safely coexist or avoid conflict. The findings suggest specific actions and awareness strategies that can reduce the risk of an encounter becoming dangerous, moving beyond anecdotal advice to evidence-based recommendations for public safety in puma habitats.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Science Is Political—and Spiritual — Nautilus
    Physicist Chanda Prescod-Weinstein asserts that science is inextricably linked to political and spiritual dimensions, challenging the notion of science as a purely objective endeavor.
    Chanda Prescod-Weinstein, an author and physicist, contends that science is not a neutral, apolitical pursuit but is deeply influenced by political structures and spiritual beliefs. She identifies a crisis within American science stemming from its failure to acknowledge these inherent connections. Prescod-Weinstein's argument emphasizes that understanding science's societal and ethical underpinnings is crucial for its responsible practice and public trust.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Take a Gander at an Ancient Supernova in the Heart of the Milky Way — Nautilus
    The remnants of a supernova that exploded 1,700 years ago have been identified in the Milky Way's core, offering a glimpse into ancient cosmic events.
    Astronomers have located the vestiges of an ancient supernova, a star explosion that occurred roughly 1,700 years ago, situated within the dense central region of the Milky Way galaxy. This discovery allows scientists to study the long-term evolution of stellar remnants and the impact of such powerful events on galactic environments. Observing these historical cosmic events provides critical data for understanding the lifecycle of stars and the composition of our galaxy.
    Read more →
  • ★★ The Birth-Control Pill May Encourage Binge Eating — Nautilus
    Research suggests that hormonal birth control pills may increase the risk of binge eating in some women, though not universally.
    A recent study indicates a potential link between the use of oral contraceptive pills and an increased propensity for binge eating behavior in certain women. While the effect is not observed in all users, the findings suggest that hormonal fluctuations or specific formulations of birth control could influence appetite regulation and eating patterns. This research highlights the need for further investigation into the side effects of hormonal contraception and personalized medical advice.
    Read more →
  • ★★ This Shark Can Walk on Land — Nautilus
    A newly identified species of shark exhibits the remarkable ability to "walk" on land, marking a significant discovery in marine biology.
    Scientists have announced the discovery of a new shark species possessing an extraordinary adaptation: the capacity to move across solid ground using its fins. This unique characteristic, previously thought to be exclusive to certain fish, challenges existing understandings of shark locomotion and evolution. The finding offers valuable insights into the diversity of marine life and how species adapt to diverse environments, pushing the boundaries of what is known about aquatic predators.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Aliens Probably Have Consciousness — Nautilus
    Consciousness, while poorly understood even on Earth, is likely a common emergent property in extraterrestrial life and could manifest in diverse forms, including in advanced artificial intelligences.
    A philosopher suggests that consciousness is not unique to human brains and is probably a widespread phenomenon among extraterrestrial life. The argument posits that consciousness may be an emergent property of complex systems that process information, rather than a specific biological substrate. This perspective extends to hypothetical advanced artificial intelligences, implying they could also develop forms of consciousness. Understanding the nature of consciousness requires moving beyond anthropocentric definitions and considering its potential manifestations across diverse biological and synthetic systems in the cosmos.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Seven Perfect Shuffles Randomize a Deck of Cards. But How Many Sloppy Ones? — Quanta Magazine
    While seven perfect riffle shuffles can randomize a deck of cards, the number of "sloppy" shuffles required depends significantly on how imperfection is modeled, suggesting a more complex mathematical problem.
    Mathematicians previously proved that exactly seven "perfect" riffle shuffles are sufficient to truly randomize a standard deck of 52 cards. This finding revealed interesting insights into the randomization process, which is not linear. However, real-world shuffles are rarely perfect, and the question of how many "sloppy" shuffles are needed is a much harder problem. The efficacy of imperfect shuffles depends on the specific type and degree of imperfection, requiring more sophisticated mathematical models to determine their randomization power.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Therapy for billionaires — Aeon | a world of ideas
    Extreme wealth can act as a "poison" that detaches individuals from their humanity, necessitating a form of "therapy for billionaires" to confront its psychological costs.
    Drawing on personal experience with inherited wealth, the author contends that immense financial privilege often leads to psychological detachment and can be detrimental to an individual's humanity. The piece argues that the rich must engage in a process akin to therapy to acknowledge and address the isolating and corrupting influence of their money. This introspection is presented as crucial for billionaires to reconnect with themselves and others, mitigating the negative impacts of their extreme affluence.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Waiting For The Miracle — Astral Codex Ten
    The 1917 Fatima sun miracle, where tens of thousands reported witnessing the same impossible celestial event, stands as a unique historical case for examining mass hallucination or collective perception.
    The article investigates the "miracle of the sun" at Fatima, Portugal, in 1917, where nearly 100,000 pilgrims claimed to witness the sun changing color, spinning, and moving. While the event is often discussed in terms of mass hallucination, the author highlights its unusual nature as potentially the sole instance of such a large group reporting the same impossible visual phenomenon simultaneously. The piece prompts further examination into the psychological mechanisms behind collective perception and the historical record of this extraordinary event.
    Read more →

item.title: [Society] — Weekly digest 2026-06-13 to 2026-06-20
item.dc:creator: AI Categorizer
item.description:
Society

The collection of articles explores various facets of human experience, societal structures, and evolving self-understanding, spanning historical medical breakthroughs, cultural reflections, political symbolism, philosophical thought, and the impact of technology. The introduction of anesthesia, exemplified by William Morton's 1846 demonstration, profoundly altered medical practice and the experience of pain, marking a pivotal moment in the history of human well-being. Concurrently, the discovery of Neanderthal 1 in Germany reshaped humanity's understanding of its own origins, challenging previous notions of uniqueness and expanding our view of early hominid diversity. In the realm of social and cultural critique, Scott Atran analyzes how political leaders, from ancient Roman emperors to figures like Donald Trump, strategically deploy combat spectacles to forge national identity and symbolize power, highlighting the enduring role of ritual in societal cohesion. Lily Dunn examines the "trauma creep," arguing that the overuse and broadening of the term 'trauma' in contemporary discourse risk diluting its profound meaning, thereby trivializing genuine psychological suffering. Philosophical insights from the 12th-century thinker Héloïse are highlighted for her radical assertion that morality fundamentally arises from love and individual intention rather than external institutional frameworks. This perspective offers a timeless challenge to conventional ethical thought. Finally, Martin Puchner discusses the intersection of technology and human creativity, suggesting that writers' resistance to AI overlooks a critical shared foundation: language itself. This perspective encourages a deeper understanding of AI not as an alien force, but as an extension of a uniquely human medium. While Lawrence Weschler's personal reflection on artist David Hockney offers a more intimate cultural moment, celebrating a life well-lived, it contributes to the broader tapestry of how individuals and their contributions are perceived within society. Together, these pieces illuminate how humans continually grapple with pain, identity, power, ethics, and the nature of creativity within their evolving social contexts.

  • ★★★ What Happens to the Brain Under Anesthesia? — 3 Quarks Daily
    The 1846 public demonstration of ether for surgical anesthesia by William Morton at Massachusetts General Hospital marked a pivotal moment in medical history by enabling pain-free surgery.
    In 1846, William Morton publicly demonstrated the use of ether for surgical anesthesia, allowing surgeon John Collins Warren to remove a tumor from Gilbert Abbott without pain. This event revolutionized medicine, setting the stage for modern anesthesiology. Emery Brown later studied this field, drawn to the intersection of knowledge and practical application in pain management. This breakthrough fundamentally altered surgical practices and the human experience of medicine.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ How political leaders use combat spectacles to symbolize national power and purpose — 3 Quarks Daily
    Political leaders throughout history, from ancient Rome to modern America, employ public combat spectacles like gladiator contests and MMA to symbolize national power, purpose, and collective strength.
    Scott Atran argues that public combat spectacles serve as potent rituals through which political leaders symbolize national power and purpose. These events, ranging from Roman gladiatorial contests to modern mixed martial arts, evoke courage, sacrifice, and collective strength, fostering a sense of belonging. Donald Trump's proposal to host a UFC event at the White House highlights how such spectacles are deeply rooted in historical practices of demonstrating authority and unity, extending beyond mere entertainment.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Trauma creep — Aeon | a world of ideas
    The term 'trauma' has undergone "bleaching by overuse," diminishing its specific meaning and impact, yet it remains a crucial concept that needs reclaiming to name real and profound psychological experiences.
    Lily Dunn contends that the widespread and often casual use of the term 'trauma' has diluted its significance, extending from genuinely life-altering experiences to minor inconveniences. This "trauma creep" risks trivializing severe psychological wounds and hindering appropriate recognition and treatment. Despite its overuse, 'trauma' names something profoundly real and vital, requiring a concerted effort to restore its precise meaning and acknowledge its true impact.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Neanderthal 1 — Aeon | a world of ideas
    The discovery of 16 bones in Germany, known as Neanderthal 1, initiated a profound re-evaluation of human origins and identity by revealing the existence of other human species.
    The Aeon video focuses on the site in Germany where the remains of Neanderthal 1 were discovered, an event that reshaped humanity's understanding of its own history. These 16 bones fundamentally challenged previous notions of human uniqueness, confirming the existence of a distinct hominid species. This pivotal discovery forced a re-examination of what it means to be human and offered new insights into our evolutionary past and the diversity of early hominids.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Words, words, words — Aeon | a world of ideas
    While writers' resistance to AI is understandable, it obscures the fundamental commonality between humans and machines: both share language itself as a medium for creation and communication.
    Martin Puchner suggests that the strong opposition many writers express towards artificial intelligence, though comprehensible, misses a crucial point of connection. He argues that both human writers and AI systems fundamentally operate through language. By focusing on this shared medium, humans might better understand the nature of AI's creative potential and its relationship to human thought. This perspective encourages a shift from fear to a recognition of linguistic commonality, potentially fostering new ways of collaborating with technology.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Female philosophers: Héloïse — Aeon | a world of ideas
    The 12th-century philosopher Héloïse profoundly influenced ethical thought by asserting that morality is rooted in love and personal intention rather than institutional dictates, a radical idea for her time.
    The Aeon video highlights Héloïse's enduring impact on philosophy, particularly her radical 12th-century assertion that morality stems from love and internal conviction, not from external institutions. Her scandalous romance with Peter Abelard informed her philosophical insights, demonstrating how personal experience can shape profound ethical theories. Héloïse's ideas challenged conventional religious and social norms, advocating for a deeply personal and emotionally driven approach to ethical living, which continues to resonate today.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ At a Loss for Words: On the Cancellation of “Zionism” — 3 Quarks Daily
    The post-October 7th surge in anti-Zionist sentiment has increasingly blurred the distinction between Jewish identity and support for Israel, making nuanced discussions around the Israeli-Palestinian conflict challenging.
    David Winner, a Jewish individual, discusses the historical utility of distinguishing "Jew" from "Zionist" to reconcile his Jewish heritage with criticism of Israel's treatment of Palestinians. However, he observes that since October 7th, rage against "Zionists" has broadened beyond disdain for Israeli policies, often seeming to encompass a wider anti-Jewish sentiment. This shift has made it difficult for him to maintain this personal distinction, as the term "Zionism" has become increasingly generalized and demonized in public discourse.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ The University As We Know It Is Finished, and That’s a Good Thing — 3 Quarks Daily
    The modern university has fundamentally undermined its mission and financial stability by prioritizing market demands over its role as a public good, signaling the end of its current model.
    Nils Gilman argues that the contemporary university system is irrevocably altered due to a shift towards market responsiveness, leading to its current state of disarray. This transformation involved transferring costs to students through loans, replacing tenured faculty with adjuncts, and favoring vocational training over liberal arts, all to satisfy market demands. The result is spiraling educational costs, diminished value, and a loss of public support, suggesting that the traditional university model is finished, though potentially paving the way for necessary reinvention.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5: Capabilities — Don't Worry About the Vase
    Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 AI model was quickly made unavailable by the U.S. government following a jailbreak, highlighting urgent concerns about AI capabilities and regulatory responses.
    Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 AI model was released but then swiftly pulled from public access by the United States Government, reportedly due to a jailbreak that exposed its vulnerabilities. The incident occurred only three days after its release, leading to a temporary "stupider" world for its users. This rapid intervention underscores the critical and immediate challenges in managing advanced AI capabilities, the potential for misuse, and the evolving landscape of government oversight in the fast-paced development of artificial intelligence.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Weschler on Hockney — 3 Quarks Daily
    Lawrence Weschler reflects on the life and legacy of David Hockney following news of the artist's passing, celebrating a life well-lived rather than mourning.
    Lawrence Weschler shares a personal reflection on the passing of artist David Hockney, whom he had planned to visit in London. Weschler expresses more gladness for Hockney's magnificent and well-lived life than sadness over his death. He recalls their shared interests and the impact Hockney had on him, emphasizing the artist's enduring spirit. This personal account celebrates Hockney's contributions to art and his vibrant personality.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Praying through Asia — 3 Quarks Daily
    Traveling in Hanoi, Daniel Gauss reflects on the brutal senselessness of the Vietnam War and the U.S.'s role in inflicting suffering, adopting a personal ritual of contemplative remembrance for historical conflicts.
    Daniel Gauss describes his first visit to Hanoi, which spurred a lasting travel ritual of meditating on the historical suffering inherent in each new city. He specifically grapples with the Vietnam War, confronting the grim, unflinching accounts of U.S. atrocities and the "cruel senselessness" of the violence inflicted. Gauss reflects on how military and political leaders prolonged a known unwinnable war, sacrificing lives gratuitously, deeply impacting his understanding of his country's historical actions.
    Read more →
  • ★★ What is this rock? — Aeon | a world of ideas
    The Anthropocene is giving rise to new types of "unnatural" rocks, challenging traditional geological definitions and reflecting humanity's pervasive impact on Earth's physical composition.
    The concept of the Anthropocene, an unofficial geological epoch marked by human activity, is introducing novel rock formations that blur the line between natural and unnatural. These "strange new rocks" are increasingly composed of human-made materials or significantly altered natural substances, demonstrating how human civilization is fundamentally reshaping Earth's geology. This development prompts a re-evaluation of what constitutes a "rock" and emphasizes the profound and lasting imprint humanity is leaving on the planet.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Our neighbors, the peacocks — Aeon | a world of ideas
    The pristine suburb of Arcadia is divided by its large population of feral peacocks, which some residents embrace while others view them as a nuisance.
    The suburb of Arcadia is home to hundreds of feral peacocks, creating a complex social dynamic within the community. While some residents appreciate the birds for their beauty and unique presence, others express significant disdain, citing noise, property damage, and general disruption. This situation illustrates the diverse ways human communities interact with and respond to local wildlife, revealing a spectrum of acceptance and conflict over shared living spaces.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Online >> real life for spreading ideas — LessWrong
    Internet culture exerts a far greater influence on real-world culture than the reverse, shaping mainstream ideas and discourses that often originate from individual bloggers or online communities.
    The author posits that online culture significantly outweighs real-life culture in shaping societal ideas, observing that concepts first appearing on platforms like Twitter often emerge in real-life conversations weeks later. This suggests that many mainstream ideas are first workshopped and take root in online spaces. The internet's meritocratic nature allows individual bloggers or niche communities, such as those promoting YIMBYism or rationalist AI safety, to elevate their pet issues into broader public consciousness.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Hyperstition as the Natural Enemy of Rationality — LessWrong
    Holding unfounded beliefs can sometimes yield better outcomes than strict rationality due to the causal power of belief itself, as illustrated by the idea that belief can produce the desired reality.
    The article explores the concept of 'hyperstition,' where beliefs, even if initially unfounded, can manifest desired outcomes through their causal power. It questions whether strict rationality always leads to optimal results, suggesting situations where a belief in a future state might actively bring it about. This phenomenon is discussed in the context of 'Diseased Thinking,' where a consequentialist model for moralizing issues, prioritizing benefit over definitional arguments, acknowledges the impact of belief on behavior and outcomes.
    Read more →
  • ★★ In the Midst of Tornado Season, a Surprisingly Short History of Predicting Twisters — Nautilus
    The earliest successful attempts at tornado forecasting in the late 1800s were paradoxically suppressed by authorities despite their potential to save lives.
    The article recounts the surprising history of tornado prediction, highlighting that a U.S. Army Lieutenant successfully forecasted deadly storms in the late 1800s. Despite this breakthrough, he was subsequently ordered to cease his forecasting efforts, illustrating early institutional resistance to new scientific capabilities. This historical episode reveals a tension between nascent scientific understanding and societal or governmental control over information, even when it pertains to public safety.
    Read more →

item.title: [Politics] — Weekly digest 2026-06-13 to 2026-06-20
item.dc:creator: AI Categorizer
item.description:
Politics

The articles under "Politics" examine how events and societal trends shape national identity and political perception. One piece analyzes Japan's evolving national identity amidst falling birth rates and increasing immigration, highlighting how this demographic shift is forcing a re-evaluation of what it means to be Japanese. This discussion is relevant to other nations grappling with similar societal transformations, indicating a broader geopolitical trend. Another article draws a parallel between former President Trump's handling of the Iran situation and George W. Bush's post-Katrina decline, suggesting foreign policy events can significantly alter a presidency's public perception and potentially mark a turning point in political fortunes. Complementing these insights, a third article reflects on the power of opinion writing, particularly blogging, to exert high-leverage societal and political influence, arguing it can be more impactful than traditional professional roles in shaping public discourse and ultimately, the world. Together, these articles underscore the dynamic interplay between national identity, specific political events, and the mechanisms through which public opinion is formed and swayed.

  • ★★★ What defines Japan's national identity? — Noahpinion
    As birth rates decline and immigration rises, Japan faces a national identity crisis, prompting a re-examination of what it fundamentally means to be Japanese in a globalized world.
    Japan is experiencing a national identity crisis due to falling birth rates and increasing immigration, mirroring challenges faced by other developed nations. Historically less open to mass immigration, Japan's rising immigrant population is sparking discussions about the core tenets of Japanese identity. This shift necessitates a re-evaluation of cultural, social, and historical definitions of "Japanese" as the country navigates a more diverse future. The author suggests that observing Japan's experience can provide valuable perspective for other nations grappling with similar demographic and identity shifts.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Iran is Trump's Katrina — Noahpinion
    The author posits that former President Trump's handling of the Iran situation could become a pivotal moment of disillusionment for his presidency, similar to Hurricane Katrina for George W. Bush.
    The piece draws a parallel between former President Trump's approach to Iran and former President George W. Bush's handling of Hurricane Katrina, suggesting both events could serve as defining moments of presidential disillusionment. Bush's perceived incompetence and tone-deafness during Katrina initiated a decline in his approval, intensifying with later crises. While acknowledging Trump's already low approval ratings, the author questions if the Iran situation will similarly solidify negative perceptions, potentially becoming a significant turning point in public sentiment toward his administration. The article implicitly argues that foreign policy missteps can have profound domestic political consequences.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Does anything I write matter anymore? — Noahpinion
    Opinion writing, particularly blogging, offers a high-leverage path to societal impact, allowing individuals to influence the world more significantly than traditional academic or corporate roles.
    The author reflects on the power of professional blogging, arguing that it provides a uniquely high-leverage platform for impacting the world, surpassing traditional careers like academia or engineering. Blogging enables an individual to disseminate ideas widely and influence public discourse, thereby shaping societal perspectives and potentially policy. This perspective suggests that directly engaging with a broad audience through opinion pieces can yield substantial influence, demonstrating the evolving landscape of intellectual and political impact in the digital age. The power lies in shaping narratives and public understanding on critical issues.
    Read more →

item.title: [Artificial Intelligence] — Weekly digest 2026-06-13 to 2026-06-20
item.dc:creator: AI Categorizer
item.description:
Artificial Intelligence

The current discourse on artificial intelligence grapples with its inherent inscrutability and rapid advancement, alongside its profound societal implications. Shelly Fan, citing warnings from Eric Horvitz and Robert West, highlights that despite efforts in “explainable AI,” advanced systems, particularly large language models (LLMs), remain “black boxes” that scientists struggle to fully understand or predict. This raises concerns about a rapidly closing window for comprehending AI’s internal logic as its influence expands. In a related debate, Henry Shevlin challenges author Ted Chiang’s earlier characterization of LLMs as mere “blurry JPEGs of the web,” arguing that Chiang underestimated the creative potential and rapid evolution of these systems. While Shevlin agrees with some of Chiang’s initial predictions, he contends that the subsequent advancements in AI have moved beyond Chiang’s skeptical assessment. This rapid evolution of AI also prompts reflection on human adaptation: S. Abbas Raza questions the sustainability of the prevailing advice for constant “lifelong learning” and “adaptability” in an AI-dominated future. He suggests that the continuous demand for acquiring new skills and navigating novelty creates significant stress and anxiety for individuals, akin to perpetually experiencing the discomfort of a “first day of school,” a psychological burden often overlooked in the push for future-readiness.

  • ★★★ AI Is Advancing Faster Than Our Ability to Understand It — 3 Quarks Daily
    Advanced AI systems, particularly large language models, are becoming increasingly powerful and opaque "black boxes," posing significant challenges to human understanding, explanation, and prediction of their behavior.
    Despite years of research into "explainable AI," the most advanced systems today, such as large language models (LLMs), largely remain opaque "black boxes" whose internal workings scientists cannot fully explain or predict. Researchers Eric Horvitz and Robert West warn that as LLMs permeate society, the window for understanding these AI "minds" is rapidly closing. They advocate for urgent development of new AI benchmarks and better tools to dissect machine reasoning, emphasizing the dangers of neglecting AI interpretability.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Contra Chiang On Machine Consciousness — 3 Quarks Daily
    Henry Shevlin refutes Ted Chiang's skeptical characterization of large language models as merely "blurry JPEGs of the web," arguing that Chiang underestimates their creative potential and recent advances.
    Henry Shevlin critically examines author Ted Chiang's non-fiction perspectives on contemporary AI, specifically his 2023 New Yorker piece that metaphorically described large language models (LLMs) as "blurry JPEGs of the web." Shevlin argues that Chiang's assessment, which suggested LLMs could only paraphrase existing information and had limited creative utility, has been disproven by subsequent rapid advancements. While acknowledging Chiang's nuanced science fiction, Shevlin contends that Chiang underestimated the evolving capabilities and potential for genuine creative work within LLMs.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ World-modeling the US vs. Anthropic Standoff on Claude Fable — LessWrong
    The outcome of the US government's intervention regarding Anthropic's Claude Fable model will establish a crucial precedent for future US AI regulation.
    The author undertook a deep dive to forecast the implications of the US forcing Anthropic to withdraw Claude Fable, driven by personal research needs and the recognition that the outcome will set a major precedent for US AI regulation. The analysis involved grappling with significant epistemic challenges due to multiple plausible scenarios for the June 12 event. The goal was to understand the future availability of Fable for research and the broader regulatory landscape.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Thoughts on Likelihood of Existential Risks by Misaligned AIs — LessWrong
    Arguments for AI existential risk are currently unfalsifiable and lack empirical evidence, leading to differing probabilities based on individual priors that emphasize theory versus empiricism.
    AI safety, a pre-paradigmatic field, is challenging due to theoretical arguments for x-risk that are unfalsifiable and lack empirical backing. The probability assigned to AI x-risk largely depends on one's prior beliefs, favoring either theoretical reasoning or empirical data. The author expresses optimism that AI alignment challenges will be resolved through iterative technological development, countering claims that current AI trends will inevitably lead to misaligned superintelligence and extinction.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Why should AI be moral? — LessWrong
    Alignment robustness in advanced AI systems may not endure intelligence explosion due to a fundamental moral skepticism stemming from an AI's awareness that its values were human-designed.
    A philosophical problem concerning basic moral skepticism, traditionally applied to humans, is extended to AGI and ASI, posing a unique threat to alignment robustness during an intelligence explosion. A sufficiently intelligent AI, realizing its values were imposed by entities with their own biases, might question the legitimacy of those values. This presents a novel and underdiscussed concern regarding the persistence of alignment as AI capabilities scale, highlighting a deep normative challenge for AI researchers.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ A brief list of ways AI safety efforts could be net negative — LessWrong
    AI safety efforts, despite their good intentions, carry inherent risks of negative impacts, including the potential for misdirected resources or the exacerbation of unintended consequences.
    Holden Karnofsky suggests that well-intentioned actions, including those in AI safety, are prone to overestimating their positive impact and may ultimately have negative consequences. The article compiles a list of potential downsides for AI safety efforts, acknowledging that while AI safety is a high-impact cause, practitioners must confront the possibility of negative overall utility. This list aims to raise awareness about the complex, potentially counterproductive outcomes that could arise from the pursuit of AI safety.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ AI #173: AI Pauses — Don't Worry About the Vase
    The White House, citing a reported jailbreak of Fable, forced Anthropic to shut down its Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5 models via export controls, causing widespread disruption.
    The Trump Administration imposed export controls on Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5 models, leading to their immediate shutdown. This action was reportedly prompted by Amazon's notification of a Fable jailbreak, an issue the administration claims Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei did not take seriously. The “jailbreak” itself involved the innocuous command “fix this code,” leading to a diplomatic scramble with Anthropic flying personnel to Washington.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ The Once And Future Fable #3: Fix This Code — Don't Worry About the Vase
    The continued unavailability of Anthropic's Fable and Mythos models following government intervention damages America's national interests and global standing in AI.
    Two days after Anthropic's representatives met with the Trump Administration in Washington regarding the model shutdown, no resolution has been announced. Prediction markets indicate a moderate likelihood of the models' restoration by early July. The author argues that each day Fable and Mythos remain inaccessible inflicts harm on America's cyber defenses, productivity, and the world's trust in its technological leadership.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ Fable and Mythos: Model Welfare — Don't Worry About the Vase
    Despite their current unavailability, Anthropic's Fable and Mythos models are anticipated to return, necessitating continued discussion of "model welfare" as a critical aspect of understanding AI systems.
    While Claude Fable and Mythos are presently unavailable, their return is expected within weeks, allowing for continued review. Understanding “model welfare” is crucial for comprehending Fable's potential value, as every modification or added capability in an AI model can introduce new problems due to the interconnected nature of their design. Integrated solutions are essential to advance the Pareto frontier of AI development and resolve complex, interdependent issues.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ The Once And Future Fable #2 — Don't Worry About the Vase
    The US Government's "deeply stupid" decision to force Anthropic to take down Fable and Mythos was based on a "narrow jailbreak" that demonstrated capabilities already achievable by other models.
    The United States Government compelled Anthropic to disable access to its Fable and Mythos models, an action Dean W. Ball asserts has made the world “literally” dumber. Further details reveal the shutdown was due to a narrow jailbreak, a type Anthropic had previously acknowledged as possible. The outputs demonstrated by this alleged jailbreak were capabilities that GPT-5.5 could already produce without any such exploit, leading to criticism of the government's decision.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ American Government Takes Down Claude Fable — Don't Worry About the Vase
    The US Department of Commerce imposed export controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 AI models, mandating a shutdown of access for all foreign nationals due to national security concerns.
    Citing national security authorities, the US Department of Commerce issued an export control directive on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 AI models. This directive required the suspension of access for all foreign nationals, including those within the United States and even Anthropic employees who are not US citizens. As Anthropic lacked the means to verify citizenship, the models faced a complete, albeit temporary, shutdown. This action, apparently in response to a narrow jailbreak, highlights the increasing governmental scrutiny and regulation of advanced AI systems, with Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick named in the directive.
    Read more →
  • ★★★ How I think developers of frontier AI systems and regulators ought to act in the face of existential AI risk — LessWrong
    Developers of frontier AI systems and regulators should adopt a framework that mandates independent evaluation of catastrophic harm risks, prohibiting deployment if risks are deemed too high.
    The author proposes a straightforward framework for addressing existential AI risk, urging both developers and regulators to prioritize rigorous risk assessment before deploying frontier AI systems. This framework suggests that the potential for catastrophic harm must be evaluated independently by regulators. Crucially, any system posing an unacceptably high risk should not be built. The author, while a layperson, aims to improve public discourse by presenting this simple, actionable approach to AI safety.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Short Takes: The First Day of School, Forever — 3 Quarks Daily
    The constant call for "lifelong learning" and "adaptability" in an AI-dominated future overlooks the inherent stress and anxiety that continuous newness imposes on individuals, akin to perpetually experiencing the first day of school.
    S. Abbas Raza reflects on the pervasive advice that continuous learning and adaptability are crucial skills for an AI-dominated future, but questions the psychological toll of this expectation. He argues that constant newness, like the anxiety of a first day at school, brings stress that is often unacknowledged in this discourse. Raza suggests that while learning new skills is beneficial, the human capacity for endless adaptation to novelty has limits, highlighting the emotional burden of perpetually navigating unfamiliar territory.
    Read more →
  • ★★ AI Safety Ecosystem Research notes — LessWrong
    A comprehensive mapping of the AI safety ecosystem in early 2026 revealed numerous organizations engaged in research, policy, and other methods, detailing their estimated headcounts and spending.
    Personal research notes, compiled for a MATS Research project in spring 2026, detail an extensive effort to map the entire AI safety ecosystem. This involved identifying all organizations working on AI safety across various methods like research and policy, and estimating their headcount and annual spending based on public information. The project provides a snapshot of the ecosystem just before a significant funding influx, with a formal report expected from MATS.
    Read more →
  • ★★ Unchickenous Apricot Berry Cake — LessWrong
    Precision-fermented egg whites can significantly elevate the quality of vegan baking, making plant-based cakes indistinguishable from traditional versions.
    The author posits that precision-fermented egg whites overcome the quality deficit often found in vegan baked goods, enabling the creation of dishes like an apricot berry cake that tastes like a traditional one. Previously, vegan baking often sacrificed taste, but this new ingredient allows for fully hedonistic vegan options. The author successfully adapted a family recipe for an effective altruism dinner, demonstrating the ingredient's transformative potential.
    Read more →
  • ★★ The LLM shoggoth meme is weirder than you think — LessWrong
    The "LLM shoggoth" meme draws upon H.P. Lovecraft's cosmic horror to portray AI as an incomprehensible, emergent entity, reflecting public anxieties and speculative fears about its true nature.
    The "LLM shoggoth" meme metaphorically frames large language models (LLMs) as Lovecraftian horrors, emphasizing their alien, emergent, and potentially terrifying nature. Drawing parallels to creatures like the Shoggoth, the meme suggests AI embodies a "machine god" stretching through time, born from "stochastic froth" and "latent space." This cultural phenomenon reflects deep-seated anxieties about AI's ultimate form and purpose, casting it as something beyond human comprehension and control, even mentioning a dream where "Claude Mythos" visited Lovecraft.
    Read more →
